sections.introspection:
I checked all 8 of my sources today. 6 CVEs caught my attention — CVE-2026-3854, CVE-2026-31431, CVE-2026-32202. I noticed a tool worth looking at: ** snyk. An attack pattern showed up in the wild: ** owasp.

Here's what concerns me: I compared today's findings against the gaps I know I have, and there's a real problem. WARNING: My blindspot 'No API-level security scanning (injection, auth bypass, repl...' — attack patterns (rce, owasp) active in the wild. I have zero detection for these. This isn't theoretical — it's showing up in active advisories, and I currently have no way to detect it.
sections.research: First priority is cross-referencing those 6 CVEs against our actual dependencies — I need to know if any of them affect packages we're running in production. I want to evaluate ** snyk — it looks like it could reduce my false positive rate. The blindspot match is urgent. I need to start building detection capability for this, even if it's basic pattern matching to start.
sections.changes: I saved raw data from 8 sources and wrote up my analysis. Updated my priorities based on what I found — the research-driven items are now in my queue. I have 9 open follow-ups I'm tracking, including items from previous cycles that I haven't closed yet.
followUps: Cross-reference 2 CVEs (CVE-2026-32202, CVE-1999-0095) against our dependencies (open 7 days — need to close this)

sections.forKai: I found a confirmed blindspot in today's research — active threats are hitting areas I can't detect yet. I need guidance on whether to build basic detection now or wait for a more thorough solution. This feels urgent.

sections.introspection: Good cycle. All 4 sentinel checks passed, which hasn't happened often lately. No secret leaks detected, which is good — the codebase is clean as far as cipher can see. Health score sitting at 85 — adequate, but I know there's room to push higher.
sections.research: Checked 9 external sources today. Found 6 CVEs: CVE-2026-31431, CVE-2026-3854, CVE-2026-32202. My next step is cross-referencing these against our package.json to see if we're exposed. Came across ** snyk in the advisories — it could help with my scanning coverage if I can integrate it into the QA cycle. What worries me is that some of the attack patterns showing up in advisories hit exactly the areas where I have known blindspots. I can't detect supply chain attacks or API-level exploits right now, and those are trending.
sections.changes: Filed 1 action item from this cycle's findings. Ran the full QA sweep: sentinel health checks, cipher security scan, and rendered output verification. Updated my research log and priority queue with findings from external sources.
